narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid family background in the lower-middle income bracket and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 50.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection—it's designed to flag higher-risk profiles to ensure students are set up for long-term success in law. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key aspects of your academic profile compared to typical applicants. Your LSAT score of 24 stood out as the biggest push toward the failure prediction, since it's notably lower than the average of around 37 seen in the dataset of past bar takers. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 also contributed significantly in that direction, being well below the typical 5.5 average and signaling weaker performance relative to peers. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other factor, likely because students from similar backgrounds have historically performed well. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 provided a modest boost toward passing, as it was somewhat below average but still helpful, while your Year 1 decile of 4 and full-time enrollment status offered smaller positive influences aligned with common successful patterns. Overall, your profile sits below typical students on key academic metrics like LSAT and later-year grades, which outweighed the strengths in your background. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 37 average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and highlighting any unique experiences from your full-time plans or family context in a reapplication. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these core areas—keep pushing, as your determination as a white male from a grounded background positions you well for improvement. We're here to support your next steps.
